content.text: Model capability determines which tasks a model can perform reliably and at what quality level. Choosing the right model requires understanding what "capability" actually measures — which is not a single score but a profile across dimensions like reasoning depth, instruction-following fidelity, context length, multi-modal support, and task-specific accuracy — and then matching that profile to the application's requirements.

attrs.alt: Model capability tiers: frontier models (highest capability, highest cost/latency), mid-tier models (strong capability, moderate cost), small/fast models (fast and cheap, suitable for high-volume simple tasks). Route tasks to the appropriate tier.

content.text: Coding agents, theorem proving, complex analysis
Adherence to format constraints, role restrictions, negative instructions
Max tokens the model can attend to per request
Document Q&A, long-form summarisation, code review of large files
Correctness of knowledge-grounded claims; hallucination rate
Medical/legal Q&A, technical documentation, knowledge-intensive tasks
Correctness, style, test coverage of generated code
Developer tools, automated code review, test generation
Document understanding, visual Q&A, chart analysis
Quality across non-English languages; low-resource language support

Benchmark scores are the most widely cited capability signals, but they have important limitations. Models are often fine-tuned to perform well on well-known benchmarks, inflating their apparent capability relative to performance on real tasks. The most reliable signal is performance on a task-specific evaluation set assembled from actual production inputs.
MCQ format doesn't reflect generation tasks; contamination risk
Python code generation, tested by running unit tests
Simple functions; doesn't measure multi-file or large-codebase coding
Competition maths is narrow; doesn't generalise to applied maths
Multi-turn instruction following, rated by humans or GPT-4
Holistic scores mask per-task variation; judge model bias
Graduate-level science Q&A; challenging compositional reasoning
Narrow domain coverage; saturating on frontier models

The most actionable capability signal is a custom evaluation on a sample of the actual task the model will perform in production. This requires building a small eval set, running candidate models on it, and scoring the outputs.

Context window size determines how much text the model can process in a single call. It is measured in tokens (roughly 0.75 words per token for English). A model with a 200,000-token context window can process approximately 150,000 words — about 500 pages — in one call.

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

# Context windows for common models (as of mid-2025)
CONTEXT_WINDOWS = {
    # Anthropic
    "claude-haiku-4-5-20251001": 200_000,
    "claude-sonnet-4-6":         200_000,
    "claude-opus-4-8":           200_000,
    # OpenAI
    "gpt-4o-mini":               128_000,
    "gpt-4o":                    128_000,
    # Google
    "gemini-2.0-flash":        1_000_000,  # 1M token context
}


def check_fits_context(text: str, model: str = "claude-haiku-4-5-20251001") -> dict:
    """
    Check whether a document fits in the model's context window.
    Returns token count and whether it fits (with headroom for the response).
    """
    result = client.messages.count_tokens(
        model=model,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": text}],
    )
    tokens = result.input_tokens
    limit = CONTEXT_WINDOWS.get(model, 128_000)
    response_headroom = 2_000
    fits = tokens + response_headroom <= limit

    return {
        "tokens": tokens,
        "context_limit": limit,
        "fits": fits,
        "utilisation_pct": round(tokens / limit * 100, 1),
    }


# Example: check if a large document fits
sample = "word " * 50_000  # ~50k words ≈ 65k tokens
result = check_fits_context(sample, "claude-haiku-4-5-20251001")
print(result)
# → {'tokens': ~65000, 'context_limit': 200000, 'fits': True, 'utilisation_pct': ~32.5}

Not all context windows are created equal. A model may advertise a 128k context window but degrade in recall quality for facts buried in the middle of long contexts. The "needle-in-a-haystack" test measures this: a specific fact is inserted at varying positions in a large document, and the model is asked to recall it.

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()


def needle_in_haystack_test(
    needle: str,
    needle_answer: str,
    context_size_words: int = 10_000,
    position_pct: float = 0.5,  # 0.0 = start, 0.5 = middle, 1.0 = end
) -> bool:
    """
    Test whether a model can retrieve a specific fact (needle) from a long
    document at a given depth position.
    """
    # Build padding text (filler)
    filler = ("This is filler content that does not contain the answer. " * 200)
    total_filler_words = context_size_words
    insert_at = int(total_filler_words * position_pct)

    filler_words = filler.split()
    prefix = " ".join(filler_words[:insert_at])
    suffix = " ".join(filler_words[insert_at:total_filler_words])
    document = f"{prefix}\n\n{needle}\n\n{suffix}"

    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=50,
        messages=[
            {"role": "user", "content": document},
            {"role": "user", "content": "What is the special fact mentioned in the document? Answer in one sentence."},
        ],
    )
    answer = response.content[0].text.lower()
    return needle_answer.lower() in answer


# Test retrieval at different positions
needle_text = "SPECIAL FACT: The magic word is 'chrysanthemum'."
for position in [0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 0.7, 0.9]:
    found = needle_in_haystack_test(needle_text, "chrysanthemum", position_pct=position)
    print(f"Position {position * 100:.0f}%: {'FOUND' if found else 'LOST'}")
Model Selection for Common Application Types
Mid-tier or frontier (for very complex docs)
Frontier (Opus, GPT-4o) for complex tasks; mid-tier for routine tasks
Small / fast or mid-tier depending on schema complexity
Frontier or mid-tier depending on quality bar
Cascade: Using Multiple Models in One Pipeline

A cascade routes requests through progressively more powerful models, using a cheap model for easy cases and escalating to a more capable model only when the simpler model's confidence is low. This achieves frontier-level quality at small-model cost for the majority of inputs.

import anthropic
import json

client = anthropic.Anthropic()


def call(prompt: str, model: str) -> str:
    resp = client.messages.create(
        model=model,
        max_tokens=512,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    )
    return resp.content[0].text


def get_confidence_and_answer(question: str, model: str) -> tuple[str, float]:
    """
    Ask the model for an answer and its self-assessed confidence.
    Returns (answer, confidence 0.0–1.0).
    """
    prompt = (
        f"{question}\n\n"
        "Answer the question. After your answer, on a new line output ONLY: "
        'CONFIDENCE: <float 0.0-1.0>'
    )
    response = call(prompt, model)
    lines = response.strip().splitlines()
    answer_lines = [l for l in lines if not l.startswith("CONFIDENCE:")]
    conf_line = next((l for l in lines if l.startswith("CONFIDENCE:")), "CONFIDENCE: 0.5")
    try:
        confidence = float(conf_line.split(":", 1)[1].strip())
    except ValueError:
        confidence = 0.5
    return "\n".join(answer_lines).strip(), confidence


def cascade(
    question: str,
    confidence_threshold: float = 0.8,
    fast_model: str = "claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
    slow_model: str = "claude-sonnet-4-6",
) -> dict:
    """Route to the slow model only if the fast model is not confident enough."""
    answer, confidence = get_confidence_and_answer(question, fast_model)

    if confidence >= confidence_threshold:
        return {"answer": answer, "model_used": fast_model, "confidence": confidence}

    # Escalate to the more capable model
    answer, confidence = get_confidence_and_answer(question, slow_model)
    return {"answer": answer, "model_used": slow_model, "confidence": confidence}


# Simple question — fast model should handle it
result = cascade("What is the capital of France?")
print(f"Model: {result['model_used']}, Confidence: {result['confidence']:.2f}")
print(f"Answer: {result['answer'][:100]}")
Choosing a model purely on benchmark leaderboard rank
Top-ranked model may underperform the task-specific second-best model
Evaluate 2–3 candidate models on a task-specific eval set
Using frontier models for all tasks
10–100× higher cost and latency than necessary for simple tasks
Route simple/high-volume tasks to small models; use frontier only where needed
Model misses facts from the middle of long documents despite fitting in the window
Run needle-in-a-haystack tests at your typical context lengths before deploying
New model releases quickly outpace current choices; cost/quality ratios shift
Abstract the model name into a config parameter; re-evaluate quarterly
Build a task-specific eval set of 20–50 representative inputs before choosing a model. Benchmark scores predict task performance poorly; real-task performance is the only reliable signal.
Always store the model name alongside each API response in logs. When a model is updated or replaced, the audit trail lets you understand which outputs were produced by which version.

Implement a cascade for mixed-difficulty workloads. Route 80% of easy requests to a fast model and escalate only the 20% that require deeper reasoning. This typically achieves 60–80% cost reduction with negligible quality loss.

Test at your actual context lengths, not just with short inputs. A model with a 200k context window may handle 10k tokens perfectly and show recall degradation at 150k tokens for your specific retrieval task.

Keep model selection behind a configuration parameter rather than hard-coding model strings. The LLM landscape changes every few months; a config-driven setup lets you swap models without code changes.

Model capability is not a single number — it is a multi-dimensional profile of reasoning depth, instruction-following fidelity, context window quality, domain accuracy, and multi-modal support. Benchmark scores correlate weakly with task-specific performance because they measure narrow, well-defined problems rather than the nuanced real-world tasks applications face. The selection protocol is: define the task, build a small eval set from production inputs, test 2–3 candidate models, and route different difficulty levels to different tiers. Re-evaluate on a regular cadence as the model landscape evolves.

A legal document review platform handles three types of requests from law firms: (1) simple clause extraction from standard contracts (e.g., "extract the termination date from this NDA"), (2) legal analysis questions requiring cross-referencing multiple clauses (e.g., "are there any indemnification clauses that conflict with local law?"), and (3) novel legal drafting requests (e.g., "draft a force majeure clause appropriate for a SaaS agreement governed by New York law"). Using a single frontier model for all three types would cost roughly 40× more per request than necessary.

The production pipeline implements a three-tier cascade: requests are first classified by a small model (Haiku or GPT-4o-mini, cost ~$0.003/request), which routes simple extraction tasks to the small tier, analysis tasks to the mid tier, and drafting tasks to the frontier tier. Empirical measurements showed that 62% of requests could be handled by the small model with no quality loss, 31% required the mid tier, and only 7% required the frontier model. The effective cost per request dropped from $0.18 (all-frontier) to $0.019 — a 9.5× reduction.

import anthropic
from dataclasses import dataclass
from enum import Enum

client = anthropic.Anthropic()


class Complexity(Enum):
    SIMPLE = "simple"       # clause extraction, formatting
    MEDIUM = "medium"       # cross-reference analysis
    COMPLEX = "complex"     # novel drafting, strategic advice


MODEL_MAP = {
    Complexity.SIMPLE:  "claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
    Complexity.MEDIUM:  "claude-sonnet-4-6",
    Complexity.COMPLEX: "claude-opus-4-8",
}

COST_PER_MTOK = {
    "claude-haiku-4-5-20251001": {"input": 0.25, "output": 1.25},
    "claude-sonnet-4-6":         {"input": 3.0,  "output": 15.0},
    "claude-opus-4-8":           {"input": 15.0, "output": 75.0},
}


def classify_complexity(question: str) -> Complexity:
    """Use the cheapest model to classify request complexity."""
    classification_prompt = f"""Classify this legal question as SIMPLE, MEDIUM, or COMPLEX.

SIMPLE: factual extraction, date/party identification, clause lookup
MEDIUM: cross-clause analysis, compliance checks, multi-step reasoning
COMPLEX: novel drafting, strategic advice, jurisdiction-specific analysis

Question: {question}

Answer with exactly one word: SIMPLE, MEDIUM, or COMPLEX"""

    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",  # cheapest classifier
        max_tokens=10,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": classification_prompt}],
    )
    label = response.content[0].text.strip().upper()
    return Complexity[label] if label in Complexity.__members__ else Complexity.MEDIUM


def route_and_answer(question: str, document: str) -> dict:
    """Route to appropriate model tier, track cost."""
    complexity = classify_complexity(question)
    model = MODEL_MAP[complexity]

    response = client.messages.create(
        model=model,
        max_tokens=1024,
        messages=[{
            "role": "user",
            "content": f"Document:\n{document}\n\nQuestion: {question}",
        }],
    )
    answer = response.content[0].text

    # Cost tracking
    usage = response.usage
    cost_per_mtok = COST_PER_MTOK[model]
    cost_usd = (
        usage.input_tokens  * cost_per_mtok["input"]  / 1_000_000 +
        usage.output_tokens * cost_per_mtok["output"] / 1_000_000
    )

    return {
        "answer": answer,
        "model": model,
        "complexity": complexity.value,
        "input_tokens": usage.input_tokens,
        "output_tokens": usage.output_tokens,
        "cost_usd": round(cost_usd, 6),
    }


# Example usage
doc_text = """
TERMINATION. Either party may terminate this Agreement upon 30 days written notice.
IN THE EVENT OF MATERIAL BREACH: immediate termination is allowed with 5 days cure period.
GOVERNING LAW: This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the State of New York.
"""

examples = [
    "What is the notice period for termination?",
    "Does the governing law clause create any conflict with the breach provisions?",
    "Draft a force majeure clause for this agreement that covers pandemic events.",
]

for question in examples:
    result = route_and_answer(question, doc_text)
    print(f"Q: {question[:60]}...")
    print(f"   Complexity: {result['complexity']:8s} | Model: {result['model']}")
    print(f"   Cost: ${result['cost_usd']:.5f} | Tokens: {result['input_tokens']}+{result['output_tokens']}")
    print()

API latency is the primary performance bottleneck in production LLM pipelines. Frontier models typically take 5–30 seconds for a 1,000-token response (due to autoregressive generation bottlenecks at the data centre), while small models take 0.3–1.5 seconds. For real-time applications with sub-2-second latency requirements, frontier models are often unusable regardless of capability.

Token throughput scales with batch size: most providers process batched requests more efficiently than sequential requests. The Anthropic Batch API (Messages Batches) offers 50% cost reduction and higher throughput for workloads tolerant of a few hours' delay — a natural fit for document summarisation, batch classification, and offline annotation.

Rate limits are capability-independent: all tiers share the same token-per-minute and requests-per-minute limits for a given API tier. A system routing 95% of traffic to small models and 5% to frontier models should size the rate limits based on the frontier model's TPM limit, since that is typically the binding constraint.

(pip install anthropic) — the official client for the Anthropic API. Provides Messages, Batch, Streaming, and Token Counting APIs. The count_tokens() method is particularly useful for context window management before making expensive calls.

— orchestration frameworks that abstract model calls behind a common interface. Model-agnostic routing and switching is built in; you can switch between Claude, OpenAI, and Gemini by changing a model parameter.
— tracks experiment metrics including model name, token counts, latency, and cost per request. Essential for comparing models across evaluation runs.

— for production monitoring. Track p50/p95/p99 latency per model, error rates, token consumption, and cost per hour. Alert when the frontier model's fraction of total requests exceeds the expected cascade threshold.
— a unified API that aggregates multiple model providers (Anthropic, OpenAI, Google, Meta, Mistral). Useful for capability comparison across providers with a single integration.
— open evaluation frameworks for running standardised benchmarks. Use these to compare candidate models on your own task prompts, not just the public benchmark suites.

# Token counting before a potentially expensive frontier model call
import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def smart_model_select(
    system: str,
    messages: list[dict],
    small_model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
    large_model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
    context_threshold=50_000,  # use large model for long context
    max_tokens=2048,
) -> str:
    """
    Automatically choose a model based on input size.
    Small model for short contexts; large model for long ones.
    """
    # Count tokens cheaply before choosing model
    token_count = client.messages.count_tokens(
        model=small_model,
        system=system,
        messages=messages,
    )
    input_tokens = token_count.input_tokens

    model = large_model if input_tokens > context_threshold else small_model
    print(f"Input tokens: {input_tokens:,} → using {model}")

    response = client.messages.create(
        model=model,
        max_tokens=max_tokens,
        system=system,
        messages=messages,
    )
    return response.content[0].text


# Usage: short input → Haiku; long input → Sonnet
short_msg = [{"role": "user", "content": "What is 2 + 2?"}]
long_msg  = [{"role": "user", "content": "word " * 60_000}]  # ~80k tokens

answer_short = smart_model_select("You are a helpful assistant.", short_msg)
# → using claude-haiku-4-5-20251001

answer_long = smart_model_select("You are a helpful assistant.", long_msg)
# → using claude-sonnet-4-6 (long context requires mid-tier)

Model capability selection has direct security implications for applications that rely on instruction-following fidelity to enforce safety or policy constraints. Weaker models may comply with jailbreaks, follow instructions less reliably, or output policy-violating content more frequently than frontier models, even for seemingly simple tasks. If a routing policy sends ambiguous or borderline requests to a cheaper model without adequate safety evaluation, the security posture of the entire application degrades.

Reliability concerns also arise from model versioning. Providers update models silently (e.g., 'claude-sonnet-4-6' may refer to different weights at different times) or deprecate older versions with short notice. An application hard-coded to a specific model ID may face unexpected failures when that version is retired. The mitigation is to: (1) always pin the exact model version string in production, (2) monitor the provider's changelog for deprecation notices, and (3) run a regression eval on the new model version before migrating.
Never use a weaker model tier for safety-critical routing
without dedicated safety evaluation at that tier. Small models may be more susceptible to prompt injection attacks than frontier models.
(e.g., claude-haiku-4-5-20251001 not 'claude-haiku') and subscribe to provider deprecation announcements.
downstream of any model in the cascade — do not assume that a correctly routed request will produce a safe output even from a frontier model.
model calls to protect against runaway cost in case of bugs. A stuck agent loop calling a frontier model at 10 requests/second can accumulate thousands of dollars in minutes.
Log model name with every response
for auditability. If a harmful output occurs, you must be able to determine which model produced it.

Model capability is a multi-dimensional profile — reasoning depth, instruction fidelity, context window quality, factual accuracy, and multi-modal support — not a single score. Benchmark ranks correlate weakly with task-specific performance.
Always evaluate candidate models on a task-specific eval set of 20–50 representative production inputs before selecting a model for deployment.

Use cascade routing to assign simple, high-volume requests to small models (Haiku, GPT-4o-mini) and escalate only complex requests to frontier models, achieving 60–80% cost reduction with negligible quality loss.

Needle-in-a-haystack tests reveal that context window quality degrades with document length for some models; run retrieval tests at your actual working context sizes before deploying long-context pipelines.
Abstract model names behind a configuration parameter and re-evaluate on a regular cadence (quarterly). The LLM landscape changes rapidly and cost/quality ratios shift with new releases.
Pin exact model version strings in production (never use aliases like 'claude-sonnet-latest'), log model names with every response, and monitor for provider deprecation announcements.

Security and safety properties are capability-dependent: weaker models may comply with jailbreaks more readily. Never use a weaker tier for safety-critical decisions without dedicated safety evaluation at that tier.
